tweets.text:
What does it take to bring a $90 trillion trading product under CFTC oversight?
I spoke with @j0hnwang of Kalshi, who made the case that CME's own futures carry more leverage than anything Kalshi is offering US retail traders. 👀
🎧

Timestamps:
🎯 02:51 Why Kalshi chose crypto perps as its first product outside prediction markets
🔧 05:15 What a perpetual future is and why traders prefer it over options and standard futures
🤓 11:14 The metric that reveals real demand: Kalshi's ratio of trading volume to open interest
🛡️ 14:19 How Kalshi plans to onboard Americans, and how its risk model differs from Hyperliquid's auto-deleveraging
🏛️ 21:05 Wang on Terry Duffy's claim that crypto perps are 'a disaster waiting to happen'
🎓 23:17 Fidelity: Explore crypto careers that could change your future at https://t.co/8dua6AZQXA
📱 24:00 Cape: Get 33% off your first six months at https://t.co/2ZtLziY0xt
🤝 25:32 How institutions are pitching its perps to institutions — and which ones have so far done trades
🌐 33:09 How Kalshi plans to compete with Hyperliquid, Coinbase, Kraken, and Robinhood
🔍 41:14 How Kalshi attempts to prevent insider trading — and what John thinks about its rivalry with Polymarket
💰 46:16 Kalshi's roadmap after its $1 billion raise at a $22 billion valuation

tweets.text:
What happens when the government discovers it can shut down an AI model and the market doesn't react? @kaiynne says that's the real lesson from Fable's shutdown — the precedent is now set. @LucaNetz @tayvano_ @stowellporter on Uneasy Money 🎧

Timestamps:
🏦 03:16 Porter Stowell on why Coinbase's announcement day signals a real shift in how the exchange competes
💬 06:26 Luca on why Coinbase's core trading product still lags: charts that won't load and page refreshes to buy Bitcoin
🤖 17:40 Porter on why agentic payments on Base is the real sleeper from Coinbase's announcement day
📋 25:10 What "real" means in Coinbase's tokenized stocks launch and whether the custody approach actually differentiates it
💡 34:15 Luca reveals Igloo's instrument to list crypto tokens directly on the NYSE as securities
📣 41:12 Multichain Advisors: Navigate TGEs, go-to-market, and capital markets advisory at https://t.co/fsTy2PEDx6
🧠 41:54 What Fable was, why it scared security researchers, and how a reported jailbreak report ended it
⚠️ 48:36 Taylor on why Anthropic's communications with the government made the Fable export control inevitable
🏛️ 57:09 Kain on why the Fable shutdown is the 'first fracture' in AI, and what the lack of pushback means
🌍 01:00:18 Binance's EU license bid in doubt, and Kain's contrarian case that losing Europe is bullish
📉 01:07:32 Strategy sold 32 Bitcoin on June 1 then bought back 1,587: what the panic reaction revealed about Bitcoin's concentration risk

From The Economist's "AI-Econ 101" column:
"Talk to academic economists, though, and most seem oddly uninterested in studying the impact of the potentially world-changing technology. The centre of gravity in ai economics is instead shifting away from universities—and a gang of “ai-pilled economists” is leading the charge."
#economy #economics #ai #economists

tweets.text:
Wait, so Zelle (which is owned by the big banks) is launching its own stablecoin? 

Previously the banks were claiming that stablecoins would drain trillions in deposits and destroy community banks.

I guess they're OK with that so long as they're the ones doing it 🙃

Major narrative violation: The big banks ARE doing stablecoins. 

@Zelle says it is expanding to India and will launch a stablecoin later this year. https://t.co/WvUfTlg5ey

tweets.text:
In terms of personal notes:

1. Priortech owns 21% of $CAMT, ~1.35x their MC, seems more like holding co.

2. Bit Digital people kept shouting, but NAV discount is not clean $WYFI, lot of dilution + not AI parent. 

3. Wistron (3231) is the best one I've seen since $SIVE + Ayar + Wiwynn are one of my favorite trio. 

~$16.2B MC, Q1 revenue up a ridiculous amount with 144% Y/Y growth. 

Owns 35.46% of Wiwynn, which is ~0.66x of MC. I'd expect Wiwynn to keep on growing. 

4. Sin-American Silicon 5483 does own 46.64% of GlobalWafers (6488). 

MC is $3.5B, stake value is ~$7.9B, but not exactly growing too fast independently, though it's heavily trading heavily discount to NAV. 

5. Iljin Holdings owns 42.99% of Iljin Electric, it's ~$0.22B MC vs. ~$1.13B NAV holding. This was transformer/cable DC related. 

But again idk if I trust korean stocks for NAV unlock, probably better for activist investors. 

6. Simmtech Holdings was ~$0.17B MC vs. ~$1.0B of parent, which also looks ridiculous. 

So 6x+ NAV. PCB substrate related name. Also another prob don't trust korean governance type name. 

I think both $ACMR and WUS have H-Share subsidiary listing soon, and those were the biggest NAV discounts with independent growth. 

Then you have a highly successful activist going after WUS so I'd expect some middle ground there. 

So maybe I'll put more concentration into those on top of what I already own Monday, we'll see. And Wistron was growing very fast independently. 

I'm still doing research, normally this goes in shower thoughts since I haven't formally made a conclusion, but thought others might be interested.
tweets.quoted_tweet_text: @aleabitoreddit 如果找到了能告诉一下吗？谢谢

Jason explains why HYPE’s bull case is more about flows than fundamentals.

“Valuation can play a part, but that’s not what drives prices. What drives prices higher or lower is more aggressive buyers or sellers... and how much supply exists.” https://t.co/0ONriLhC9z

thread_tweets.text: @Hanzentrade Every innovation requires navigating complexity (technology, legal, etc). If it's difficult, then solving it creates value.
thread_tweets.full_text: @Hanzentrade Every innovation requires navigating complexity (technology, legal, etc). If it's difficult, then solving it creates value.

tweets.text:
Agree - everything in hard tech is more difficult than software (especially in bio where we barely understand how it works)

Arguably the best way to do a biotech company is:
1. make a billion dollars in software
2. self-fund the biotech (at least in part, to de-risk it)

If you don't have a software billionaire patron, the uncanny valley (series C crunch) can be brutal in bio.

It's still worth attempting hard things that can move society forward even if difficult tho. Arguably you have a responsibility to do so once you've made money in software.
tweets.quoted_tweet_text: I think that tech people tend to underestimate the complexity of most medical/biological problems by 1-2 orders of magnitude.

2. self-fund the biotech (at least in part, to de-risk it)

thread_tweets.text:
I'd also say most biotechs are not as well run (from what I can tell). And they could learn a lot from the software industry.

i.e. techbio > biotech

And the 1-2 orders of magnitude complexity will get tackled with AI, not human brains.

i.e. bio-naive > bio-insight driven

tweets.text:
Money managers have abandoned positioning for $200 oil ... does this now increase the risk of $200 oil?
-----
The CFTC was closed today, so the Commitment of Traders report for June 16 will be released on Monday.

But the Intercontinental Exchange released its data for London's Brent Crude Oil Contract for June 16 today.

Here is the net (long-short) for managed money (aka "money managers").

The chart starts on April 30, 2020 (beginning of the post-COVID recovery).

In the last four weeks, these managers went from a fairly long position (see the May 19 position in the bottom panel) to a fairly low net long position (June 16). Note that these managers are rarely net short (below zero).

@CommodMkt @Rory_Johnston

Sonic announced that I am resigning from its board. I wish the new team well.  

I’ve published a factual clarification of my role and responsibilities.  

For the past 18 months, my primary focus has been building @flyingtulip_. That work continues.  We have scaled to ~$70m TVL across all 3 systems, earning ~16% APY on USDC/USDT on Ethereum and ~8% on Sonic.

Arcium has consistently been the #1 builder-centric privacy ecosystem
​
- @UmbraPrivacy all things privacy application, private transfer, swaps, DeFi, vesting/streaming
- @AviciMoney private neobank with private IRL spending & financial services
- @craftsdev private tokenized equity fundraising platform
- @meleemarkets — private prediction market, encrypted bets, avoiding front-running, copy-betting, herding & bias
​
And a whole lot more
​
Gonna be exciting to see more interesting privacy use cases powered by Arcium MPC + encrypted AI this year

tweets.text:
@lightninglu10 @luke_metro I think we agree then.

Let me flip it for you. If I can't find the spike and I'm trying. They aren't pitching it well, what is anyone supposed to do?

thread_tweets.text:
@lightninglu10 @luke_metro I think we agree then.

Let me flip it for you. If I can't find the spike and I'm trying. They aren't pitching it well, what is anyone supposed to do?
thread_tweets.full_text:
@lightninglu10 @luke_metro I think we agree then.

Let me flip it for you. If I can't find the spike and I'm trying. They aren't pitching it well, what is anyone supposed to do?

thread_tweets.text:
I've been a founder 2x, both with good outcomes so I'll say in my experience, that's not quite true. The founders job is a build a generational company. It's a subtle but important distinction.

A VCs job also isn't to find the spike in any individual pitch. It's to find the spike enough times across many pitches you can return the fund. Not 100% of the time. I don't think anyone can do that. This is why all of us have an anti portfolio.

The reality is that most teams don't have a spike. And there's also the argument too that if a team isn't able to pitch themselves, they might just not be the right team since they'll fail at pitching customers or employees. I will say I find that argument to not be a rule or complete truth, but is generally good directional guidance and true more often than not.
